title: FINMA publishes 'stable coin' guidelines
summary: On 11 September 2019, FINMA published a supplement to its ICO guidelines addressing so-called 'stable coins' -- tokens designed to minimise price fluctuation by being backed with assets such as fiat currencies, commodities, real estate or securities. FINMA describes its approach as principle-based and technology-neutral, assessing each stable coin by its economic function and purpose ('substance over form') and applying 'same risks, same rules' to the specific features of each project; depending on backing and holder rights, anti-money-laundering, securities-trading, banking, fund-management or financial-infrastructure law can all be relevant. The guidance also confirms FINMA received a request from the Geneva-based Libra Association to assess its planned Libra project, indicating it would require a payment system licence under the Financial Market Infrastructure Act, with anti-money-laundering duties applying across the ecosystem.
why_it_matters: FINMA here moves from general 'stable coin' classification principles to an indicative ruling on a single, named project (Libra) with a planned international scope, showing that it addresses such projects under existing Swiss law -- financial market infrastructure, anti-money-laundering, banking and securities rules -- rather than a bespoke stablecoin statute, applying its 'same risks, same rules' principle to the specific facts of the project.

citations.quote: the focus is on the economic function and the purpose of a token
